result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I have been thinking about maps lately — not the GPS-rendered, satellite-verified, constantly-updating kind that live in our phones, but the older sort. The hand-drawn kind. The kind made by people who had walked somewhere, looked around, tried to remember what they saw, and then translated that memory into lines on parchment or vellum or the back of whatever was available.

What strikes me about old maps is not their inaccuracy, though that is striking enough. It is their *confidence*. A medieval cartographer would draw a coastline he had never seen, extrapolating from a sailor's drunk account, and render it in the same firm ink as the coastline he had measured himself. There was no cartographic notation for *I have no idea, honestly*. There was no symbol for *a man told me this in a tavern and he seemed mostly sober*. The coastlines of imagination and the coastlines of experience looked identical on the page.

This bothers me in a way I find difficult to fully articulate, and yet I keep returning to it. I think it bothers me because it is *honest*, in a way, even while being literally dishonest. The mapmaker was doing what all of us do constantly: treating our best guesses as established facts because we have to. Because you cannot navigate by a map that is honest about its own uncertainty. A map that said *here there might be land or water, we're not really sure* would be useless to a sailor. So the cartographers lied in the service of utility, and sailors died on the rocks they had confidently drawn as open sea, and the world was slowly, bloodily, corrected.

---

I want to talk about forgetting, which is something I find more interesting than remembering, though remembering gets almost all the cultural attention.

We have memory palaces and mnemonic devices and entire industries built around helping people retain information. We have journals and diaries and photo albums and, now, the relentless archival project of social media, which promises to remember everything so that we don't have to. We treat forgetting as failure. As loss. As something to be prevented or mourned.

But I am not sure this is right.

Consider what forgetting actually does. When you forget the specific words of an argument you had with someone ten years ago, what you are left with is the *shape* of the argument — who you both were, what mattered to each of you, the emotional temperature of the relationship at that time. The details dissolve and leave behind something more like understanding than information. You know something about that person, about yourself, about the dynamic between you, that you could not have extracted from a transcript. The forgetting did the work of distillation.

Or consider skills. When you learn to drive, at first you are intensely aware of every movement — the pressure of your foot on the pedal, the angle of your wrist on the wheel, the distance to the car ahead. You are, in a sense, remembering instructions in real time. But as you become a competent driver, all of this disappears. You forget the steps because you have *become* them. The forgetting is the proof of mastery. If you could still consciously recall each instruction as you executed it, you would not be a good driver; you would be a distracted one.

What we call expertise might be, in large part, a kind of organized forgetting. The expert has processed so much information that she no longer needs to consciously access most of it. It has sunk below the waterline of awareness. It has become the shape of how she thinks rather than the content of what she thinks about.

---

There is a condition called hyperthymesia, or highly superior autobiographical memory, in which a person can recall almost every day of their life in specific, verifiable detail. You can name a date — say, April 14th, 1987 — and someone with hyperthymesia can tell you what day of the week it fell on, what the weather was like where they lived, what they ate for breakfast, what was on the news.

You might think this sounds wonderful. I used to think it sounded wonderful.

The people who have it tend to describe it as something closer to a burden. They are involuntarily returned to painful memories with the same force as pleasant ones. They cannot choose what surfaces. They live in an unusually thick present, weighted with the accumulated specificity of everything that has ever happened to them. Several of them have described feeling *trapped* — not liberated by total recall, but imprisoned in it.

This suggests something important: that forgetting is not just a failure of a system that would ideally retain everything. Forgetting is part of the design. It is how the mind makes room. It is how we avoid being crushed by the literal weight of our own experience.

The question, then, is not how to remember more, but how to forget *well*.

---

I am not sure anyone has written a good guide to forgetting well, and I think this is because it is genuinely difficult to prescribe. The things we want to forget tend to be exactly the things that resist forgetting most stubbornly. Trauma, embarrassment, loss — these burn themselves into memory with a ferocity that ordinary experience cannot match. The brain, which evolved under conditions of constant threat, has decided that painful things are important things, and it holds onto them with both hands.

So the useful forgetting — the distillation, the mastery, the clearing of space — happens mostly without our permission, to things we were not particularly trying to hold onto. And the useless forgetting — the name of a person you were just introduced to, the word you were about to say, the reason you walked into this room — happens equally without our permission, to things we would very much like to retain.

We do not, it seems, have a great deal of control over any of this.

And yet. I think there is something like a practice of forgetting that can be cultivated, though I want to be careful not to oversell it.

What I mean is something like this: there is a difference between the memory of an event and your *relationship* to that memory. You may not be able to make yourself forget something that happened. But you can, over time, change how much of your present self is organized around it. You can refuse to rehearse it. You can decline to return to it when you are not required to. You can, gradually, let it become something that happened rather than something that is still happening.

This is not suppression, which involves the active effort not to think about something — an effort that, as any psychologist will tell you, tends to make you think about it more. It is more like redirection. Like choosing to build new things rather than guard old ruins.

The ruins are still there. They are part of the landscape. But you are no longer standing in them.

---

I want to return to maps for a moment, because I think there is something here about the relationship between memory and self.

When a cartographer draws a map, he is not just recording geography. He is making an argument about what matters. He draws rivers and roads and cities, and he leaves out the individual trees, the specific boulders, the exact quality of light on a certain afternoon. This selection is not neutral. It reflects what he believes navigation requires. It reflects a theory of relevance.

Memory works similarly. When you remember your childhood, you are not retrieving a recording. You are reconstructing a map — one that reflects your current theory of who you are and how you became that person. The cartography of the self. And like all maps, it selects, simplifies, and sometimes confidently draws in coastlines that may or may not be there.

This is why memory is not reliable, a fact that makes people uncomfortable when they encounter it. Eyewitness testimony is notoriously inaccurate. People misremember where they were when they heard important news, the color of a car, the words someone said. They are not lying, in most cases. They are navigating by maps that have been, over time, quietly redrawn.

But here is the strange part: this unreliability is also what makes memory *useful* for the purposes of being a person.

If memory were a perfect recording, it would tell you everything that happened with equal weight — the afternoon you spent staring at a wall would carry the same informational density as the afternoon your child was born. You would be drowning in data with no way to know what mattered. The fact that memory is selective and reconstructive means it is *interpretive*. It is already doing the work of meaning-making. It is already, quietly, telling you what kind of story you are.

The cartographer's confident lines are not just errors. They are choices. Bets. Theories about the world dressed up as facts because that is the only way to put them to use.

---

I have been, over the past several years, interested in the specific forgetting that happens between generations.

Not the personal forgetting of individual memory, but the cultural forgetting of collective experience. The way that things which were vivid and urgent and world-shaping to one generation become mere historical fact to the next, and then become footnotes, and then become nothing — erased not by malice but by the simple passage of time and the limited bandwidth of what a culture can hold onto.

There is a woman who was born in 1920 who is still alive somewhere, probably. If she is, she has watched the living memory of World War One disappear entirely. The men who fought in those trenches, who lived with the gas and the mud and the specific, unthinkable texture of that particular horror — they are all gone. What remains is history, which is a different thing. History is a map drawn after the fact by people who were not there. It is useful. It can teach things. But it does not carry the weight of the memory itself, the way that talking to a veteran carried weight, the way his hands shook or did not shake when he mentioned certain things.

And we are, right now, watching the living memory of World War Two do the same thing. The people who remember it as experience rather than as history are in their nineties, at the youngest. Soon it will be entirely historical. Entirely cartographic. A map where all the coastlines look equally firm.

I do not think this is simply bad. I think it is genuinely complicated. Living memory of atrocity is powerful, but it is also partial and sometimes distorting. The historical record, for all its coldness, sometimes captures things that individual memory cannot — structural causes, long patterns, the view from multiple positions simultaneously. History and memory are not competitors so much as they are different tools for different purposes.

But something is lost when the last living witness goes. Something specific and irreplaceable. The difference between being told something and being in the room with someone who knows.

---

There is a word in Welsh — *hiraeth* — that has no direct English equivalent. It is usually translated as something like *longing* or *homesickness*, but these translations are inadequate. *Hiraeth* is more specifically a longing for something that may no longer exist, or may never have existed exactly as you remember it. A grief for a home you cannot return to, not merely because it is far away, but because it is gone — because the you who lived there is gone, and even if you went back to the place, the place you are nostalgic for would not be there.

I think *hiraeth* might be the emotional experience of confronting the cartographic nature of memory. The realization that the map you have been carrying is not the territory, and the territory has changed, and you cannot update the map because the territory of the past is, by definition, inaccessible.

What do you do with that?

I think you do what the best cartographers eventually learned to do: you mark the edges of what you know. You indicate, somehow, that here is where the survey ends and the extrapolation begins. You hold the map with a lighter grip, using it because you must but not mistaking it for the ground beneath your feet.

You allow yourself the *hiraeth* without letting it become a refusal to be present. You feel the loss of the place that is no longer there while still moving through the place that is.

---

I started with maps and I have ended up somewhere I did not entirely expect, which is usually the sign that the thinking has been useful.

What I believe, having followed this thread for a while, is something like this: memory and forgetting are not opposites. They are partners in the project of making a self that can move through time without being destroyed by it. We remember in order to know who we are. We forget in order to remain capable of being someone new.

The trick — and I use that word deliberately, because it is genuinely tricky, and there is no formula — is to hold this lightly. To tend the memories that orient you without being held prisoner by them. To let things go not through violence or suppression but through the gradual withdrawal of constant rehearsal. To understand that the map is not the territory, which means it is not the truth of what happened, but it is also not nothing — it is the navigational tool you have, and navigation is what you need.

Old cartographers drew sea monsters at the edges of the known world. *Here be dragons*, the maps said. Here is where our knowledge ends and our fear begins. Here is the boundary of the surveyed and the beginning of the guessed-at.

I find this more honest than it might first appear. Because the edges of what we know are real. The places where memory fails and reconstruction takes over are real. The territories we have never visited but confidently mapped anyway — they are real too, real in their influence on how we move.

Maybe the best we can do is know which parts of our maps are surveyed and which parts are dragons. Know when we are standing on solid, walked ground and when we are navigating by confidence and hope.

Know that forgetting is happening all the time, quietly, doing its work of distillation and release.

And trust, somewhat, that it knows what it is doing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
